standard_description: Use knowledge of language and its conventions when writing, speaking, reading, or listening.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: The sound of language
khan_exercises.description: Can you spot writers' use of alliteration, assonance, and onomatopoeia that add sound effects to their language?

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?** 

> Arturo __ate an atypically acrid apple.__

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *assonance*, so we want the same vowel sound (_a, e, i, o, u_) to be repeated in the sentence.

The choice that employs assonance is

>**ate an atypically acrid apple.**

options.definition: Assonance: creating a pattern by repeating initial *vowel* sounds close together, as in "Ah, the opera is awesome."

choices.content: took a bite of a baleful apple.
bit an incredibly disgusting and bitter fruit.

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?** 

> The stars in the night sky looked __beyond imagination, unreal, and bewildering.__

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *assonance*, so we want the same vowel sound (_a, e, i, o, u_) to be repeated in the sentence.

The choice that employs assonance is

>**unreal, untouchable, and unknowable.**

options.definition: Assonance: creating a pattern by repeating initial *vowel* sounds close together, as in "Ah, the opera is awesome."

choices.content: beyond imagination, beyond reason, and bewildering.

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?** 

> I am __looking forward to__ the spelling competition.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *assonance*, so we want the same vowel sound (_a, e, i, o, u_) to be repeated in the sentence.

The choice that employs assonance is

>**extremely excited for**

options.definition: Assonance: creating a pattern by repeating initial *vowel* sounds close together, as in "Ah, the opera is awesome."

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?** 

> The wind lifted Rosario’s hat off her head and flung it into a busy street where it was run over by a horse, leading her to conclude it was __sadly unsalvageable.__

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *assonance*, so we want the same vowel sound (_a, e, i, o, u_) to be repeated in the sentence.

The choice that employs assonance is

>**irredeemably irretrievable**

options.definition: Assonance: creating a pattern by repeating initial *vowel* sounds close together, as in "Ah, the opera is awesome."

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to  use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?** 

> She’s a star of stage and screen, and a major voice of her generation. We present to you: the __unforgettable__ Imogen Isley!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *assonance*, so we want the same vowel sound (_a, e, i, o, u_) to be repeated in the sentence.

The choice that employs assonance is

>**inimitable**

options.definition: Assonance: creating a pattern by repeating initial *vowel* sounds close together, as in "Ah, the opera is awesome."

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?** 


> Trent’s ability to sing two notes at once was both __awesome and off-putting.__

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *assonance*, so we want the same vowel sound (_a, e, i, o, u_) to be repeated in the sentence.

The choice that employs assonance is

>**awesome and off-putting**

options.definition: Assonance: creating a pattern by repeating initial *vowel* sounds close together, as in "Ah, the opera is awesome."

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?**

> A little red bird __perched perkily but precariously on a tree branch__. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *alliteration*, so we want the same consonant sound to be repeated at the start of words in the sentence.

The choice that employs alliteration is

>**perched perkily but precariously on a tree branch**

options.definition: alliteration: creating a pattern by repeating initial *consonant* sounds close together, as in "Kiki kicked a can carefully."

choices.content: sat perkily on a thin, slim tree branch
perched atop an alarmingly thin tree branch

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?**

> Ivan loves to hear the __all-encompassing acoustics of popcorn being made__.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *alliteration*, so we want the same consonant sound to be repeated at the start of words in the sentence.

The choice that employs alliteration is

>**crack of crisp kernels converting into popcorn**

options.definition: alliteration: creating a pattern by repeating initial *consonant* sounds close together, as in "Kiki kicked a can carefully."

choices.content: crack of crisp kernels converting into popcorn
delightful noise of popcorn being made

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?**


> Ted __absolutely aced__ his teaching exam.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *alliteration*, so we want the same consonant sound to be repeated at the start of words in the sentence.

The choice that employs alliteration is

>**tested terrifically on**

options.definition: alliteration: creating a pattern by repeating initial *consonant* sounds close together, as in "Kiki kicked a can carefully."

choices.content: was astounded at how well he did on

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?**

> __Barking and growling__, the dog lunged at me.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *alliteration*, so we want the same consonant sound to be repeated at the start of words in the sentence.

The choice that employs alliteration is

>**Barking and biting the air**

options.definition: alliteration: creating a pattern by repeating initial *consonant* sounds close together, as in "Kiki kicked a can carefully."

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?**

> __Surely__, she should redo this shoddy work!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *alliteration*, so we want the same consonant sound to be repeated at the start of words in the sentence.

The choice that employs alliteration is

>**Surely**

options.definition: alliteration: creating a pattern by repeating initial *consonant* sounds close together, as in "Kiki kicked a can carefully."

items.question_content:
**In the sentence, the author wants to use [[☃ definition 1]]. Which choice provides the best example of this?**


> You’re __so kind to resign__ your last piece of pie!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We're looking for *alliteration*, so we want the same consonant sound to be repeated at the start of words in the sentence.

The choice that employs alliteration is

>**so sweet to surrender**

options.definition: alliteration: creating a pattern by repeating initial *consonant* sounds close together, as in "Kiki kicked a can carefully."

items.question_content:
**The writer wants to use [[☃ definition 1]] to imitate the sound of the pig and the mud. Which choice best accomplishes this goal?**

> The pig __oinked, and the mud squelched under its stomping feet__.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Oink" is an onomatopoeia, so the best choice is

>**oinked, and the mud squelched under its stomping feet**

options.definition: Onomatopoeia: imitating the noise that something makes. Think of comic book sound effect noises: an explosion goes "boom"; a bouncing ball goes "boing"!

choices.content: bellowed and the mud made a sticking noise under its feet
dived into the mud and made delighted noises

items.question_content:
**The writer wants to use [[☃ definition 1]] to imitate the sound of an object hitting the ground. Which choice best accomplishes this goal?**

> Kavita watched, dismayed, as the dish fell out of her hands and __collided with__ the floor.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Smash" is an onomatopoeia, so the best choice is

>**smashed into**

options.definition: Onomatopoeia: imitating the noise that something makes. Think of comic book sound effect noises: an explosion goes "boom"; a bouncing ball goes "boing"!

items.question_content:
**The writer wants to use [[☃ definition 1]] to imitate the sound of the noise Avery is making. Which choice best accomplishes this goal?**

>  Under his breath and with his mouth closed, Avery __hummed__ a tune.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Hum" is an onomatopoeia, so the best choice is

>**hummed**

options.definition: Onomatopoeia: imitating the noise that something makes. Think of comic book sound effect noises: an explosion goes "boom"; a bouncing ball goes "boing"!

items.question_content:
**The writer wants to use [[☃ definition 1]] to imitate the sounds of the kitchen. Which choice best accomplishes this goal?**

> As he entered the kitchen, Emilio was engrossed in the __blurry activity of chefs using mixers, shifting pots from one burner to another, and rushing when timers went off__. 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Whirr", "clang", and "beep" are all onomatopoeias, so the best choice is

>**whirring of mixers, clanging of pots and pans, and the beeping of timers.**

options.definition: Onomatopoeia: imitating the noise that something makes. Think of comic book sound effect noises: an explosion goes "boom"; a bouncing ball goes "boing"!

choices.content: speed of the mixers, the sight of pots and pans changing hands, and timers sounding off.
whirring of mixers, clanging of pots and pans, and the beeping of timers.

items.question_content:
**The writer wants to use [[☃ definition 1]] to imitate the sound of the horses. Which choice best accomplishes this goal?**

> The horses __shrieked, their hooves striking__ uneasily on the road as they approached the forest.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Whinnie" and "clop" are onomatopoeias, so the best choice is

>**whinnied, their hooves clopping**

options.definition: Onomatopoeia: imitating the noise that something makes. Think of comic book sound effect noises: an explosion goes "boom"; a bouncing ball goes "boing"!

items.question_content:
**The writer wants to use [[☃ definition 1]] to imitate the sound of the wrecking ball. Which choice best accomplishes this goal?** 

> When the demolition team sent the wrecking ball sailing into the old stadium, a massive __boom__ reverberated out to the city.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
"Boom" is an onomatopoeia, so the best choice is

>**boom**

options.definition: Onomatopoeia: imitating the noise that something makes. Think of comic book sound effect noises: an explosion goes "boom"; a bouncing ball goes "boing"!

khan_exercises.title: Style and technique
khan_exercises.description: Use context to figure out how to use language appropriately-formality, irony, understatement, and overstatement.

items.question_content:
###Understatement

>*Anna goes swimming in an icy lake. When she gets out, her lips are blue and her teeth are chattering. It's obvious that she's very cold.*

**If Anna wants to use *understatement* to create emphasis, what might she say?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Understatement** is the opposite of exaggeration. However, just like exaggeration, understatement can be used to emphasize just how extreme something is.

For example:

>*You get caught outside in a rainstorm and end up soaking wet.*

If you want to emphasize how wet you are, you could

1. **Be accurate**

 *"It's raining so hard. I'm very wet."*

2. **Overstate things**

 *"It's underwater out there. I was about to drown!"*

3. **Understate things**

 *"Yeah, you could say it's a little rainy."*

Anna is very cold. If she wants to emphasize that fact by using *understatement*, she should describe herself as less cold than she actually is:

>**"It's a little chilly out here."**

choices.content: "I'm freezing! That water is so cold."

items.question_content:
###Understatement

>*Alan has been trying to beat the final boss in a new video game for over a week. After reading several walkthroughs and making dozens of attempts, he finally completes the level and wins the game.*

**If Alan wants to use *understatement* to create emphasis, what might he say?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Understatement** is the opposite of exaggeration. However, just like exaggeration, understatement can be used to emphasize just how extreme something is.

For example:

>*You get caught outside in a rainstorm and end up soaking wet.*

If you want to emphasize how wet you are, you could

1. **Be accurate**

 *"It's raining so hard. I'm very wet."*

2. **Overstate things**

 *"It's underwater out there. I was about to drown!"*

3. **Understate things**

 *"Yeah, you could say it's a little rainy."*

The video game was very hard. If Alan wants to emphasize that fact by using *understatement*, he should describe the game as less hard than it actually is:

>**"Well, that wasn't what I'd call easy."**

choices.content: "Well, that wasn't what I'd call easy."
"That was one of the hardest games I've ever played."

items.question_content:
###Understatement

>*Nessa's hockey team just added a new player. Nobody knows much about the new girl, but when she takes to the ice, it's clear she's very good. She skates skillfully down the ice, weaving between defenders, and buries the puck in the top of the net with a slap shot. Nessa skates over and gives her new teammate a high five.*

**If Nessa wants to use *understatement* to create emphasis, what might she say?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Understatement** is the opposite of exaggeration. However, just like exaggeration, understatement can be used to emphasize just how extreme something is.

For example:

>*You get caught outside in a rainstorm and end up soaking wet.*

If you want to emphasize how wet you are, you could

1. **Be accurate**

 *"It's raining so hard. I'm very wet."*

2. **Overstate things**

 *"It's underwater out there. I was about to drown!"*

3. **Understate things**

 *"Yeah, you could say it's a little rainy."*

Nessa's new teammate is very good at hockey. If Nessa wants to emphasize that fact by using *understatement*, she should describe her teammate as less talented than she actually is:

>**"So, you've played hockey before."**

items.question_content:
###Overstatement

>*Leo is out to dinner with some friends. He orders curry and takes a bite. His face turns red and he starts to sweat: it's clear that the curry is very hot.*

**If Leo wants to use *overstatement* to create emphasis, what might he say?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Overstatement** is a form of exaggeration. If we want to emphasize how extreme something is, we can do so by describing it in an over-the-top way.

For example:

>*You get caught outside in a rainstorm and end up soaking wet.*

If you want to emphasize how wet you are, you could

* **Be accurate**

 *"It's raining so hard. I'm very wet."*

* **Overstate things**

 *"It's underwater out there. I was about to drown!"*

The curry is very spicy. If Leo wants to emphasize that fact by using *overstatement*, he should describe the curry as even more spicy than it actually is:

>**"My mouth is on fire!"**

items.question_content:
###Overstatement

>*Yonas goes on a long hike that takes him up and over a mountain. When he's finished, he's soaked in sweat and clearly exhausted.*

**If Yonas wants to use *overstatement* to create emphasis, what might he say?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Overstatement** is a form of exaggeration. If we want to emphasize how extreme something is, we can do so by describing it in an over-the-top way.

For example:

>*You get caught outside in a rainstorm and end up soaking wet.*

If you want to emphasize how wet you are, you could

* **Be accurate**

 *"It's raining so hard. I'm very wet."*

* **Overstate things**

 *"It's underwater out there. I was about to drown!"*

Yonas is very tired. If he wants to emphasize that fact by using *overstatement*, he should describe himself as even more exhausted than it actually is:

>**"My legs are completely dead."**

items.question_content:
###Overstatement

>*Ellen goes to a family reunion where she sees her nephew Merle. The last time they saw each other, Merle was six years old. Now Merle is nineteen and well over six feet tall.*

**If Ellen wants to use *overstatement* to create emphasis, what might she say?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Overstatement** is a form of exaggeration. If we want to emphasize how extreme something is, we can do so by describing it in an over-the-top way.

For example:

>*You get caught outside in a rainstorm and end up soaking wet.*

If you want to emphasize how wet you are, you could

* **Be accurate**

 *"It's raining so hard. I'm very wet."*

* **Overstate things**

 *"It's underwater out there. I was about to drown!"*

Merle has grown a lot. If Ellen wants to emphasize that fact by using *overstatement*, she should describe Merle as even larger than he actually is:

>**"Holy cow, Merle, you're the size of a mountain!"**

choices.content: "Holy cow, Merle, you're the size of a mountain!"

items.question_content:
###Irony

>*Logan and Emma follow an old treasure map and find an elegantly decorated wooden chest buried in a cave. The chest is closed with an enormous iron padlock. They smash the lock with their shovels and slowly open the lid of the chest.*

**Which of the following would be an *ironic* end to this story?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Irony** occurs when there is a difference between our expectations and what really happens.

For example: 

>*A fire station burns down.*

A fire station is filled with firefighters who know all about fire prevention and management. It's the last place we'd expect to burn down. So, if one *did* burn down, it would be quite ironic.

Logan and Emma have put in a lot of effort under the expectation of discovering treasure. 

So, it would be *ironic* if 

>**The chest is completely empty.**

choices.content: The chest is filled with gold and jewels.

items.question_content:
###Irony

>*Janice and Gus are fighting over the last piece of pie.  Gus says he deserves the last piece because Janice has eaten more of this pie than he has. Janice says she deserves the last piece because Gus got to finish the last pie they shared.*

**Which of the following would be an *ironic* end to this story?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Irony** occurs when there is a difference between our expectations and what really happens.

For example: 

>*A fire station burns down.*

A fire station is filled with firefighters who know all about fire prevention and management. It's the last place we'd expect to burn down. So, if one *did* burn down, it would be quite ironic.

Janice and Gus are fighting because they both want the last piece of pie. The expectation is that one of them will get it.

So, it would be *ironic* if, because of their fight, *nobody* gets the last piece:

>**As Gus and Janice wrestle over the pie tin, the last piece of pie falls on the ground.**

choices.content: As Gus and Janice wrestle over the pie tin, the last piece of pie falls on the ground.
Gus accepts Janice's argument and lets her have the last piece.

items.question_content:
###Irony

>*Alex is worried she's going to miss her dentist appointment at 9am. To avoid this, she sets her alarm at seven in the morning, drives to her dentist's office, and arrives by 8 o'clock, a full hour early.*

**Which of the following would be an *ironic* end to this story?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
**Irony** occurs when there is a difference between our expectations and what really happens.

For example: 

>*A fire station burns down.*

A fire station is filled with firefighters who know all about fire prevention and management. It's the last place we'd expect to burn down. So, if one *did* burn down, it would be quite ironic.

Alex has put a lot of effort into arriving early to her dentist appointment. The expectation is that her attention to being on time will pay off.

So, it would be *ironic* if she were still late:

>**It turns out Alex's appointment was at 9am *yesterday*.**

choices.content: It turns out Alex's appointment was at 9am *yesterday*.
Alex makes her appointment and finds out she needs a filling.

items.question_content:
###Formal language

>*You are cordially invited to celebrate the career of Dr. Thomas Neil. To honor Dr. Neil's retirement, we will look back on his exemplary career and acknowledge how his work has impacted our lives, as well as those of people around the world.*

**Which of the following sentences matches the level of formal language in the rest of the invitation?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
As we write, we want to make sure the language we use is *consistent*. So, if we're using **formal** language, we should use similar language throughout the entire text.

Similarly, if we're using **casual** language, we shouldn't suddenly add fancy words and complicated sentence structures.

This is a formal invitation to a fancy event, so we shouldn't use casual language like "if you can make it".

The sentence that matches the rest of the text is

>***Please advise us of your ability to attend.***

choices.content: *Please advise us of your ability to attend.*
*Let us know if you can make it!*

items.question_content:
###Formal language

>*One of the great challenges of archaeology is the preservation of excavated objects. Extracting fragile artifacts from the earth requires specialized tools, delicate processes, and a great deal of patience. However, despite all these precautions, some artifacts will inevitably be broken.*

**Which of the following sentences matches the level of formal language in the rest of the text?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
As we write, we want to make sure the language we use is *consistent*. So, if we're using **formal** language, we should use similar language throughout the entire text.

Similarly, if we're using **casual** language, we shouldn't suddenly add fancy words and complicated sentence structures.

This is a formal academic text focused on archaeology, so we shouldn't use casual language like "a total bummer".

The sentence that matches the rest of the text is

>***The loss of these artifacts is a terrible tragedy.***

choices.content: *When things break, it's a total bummer.*
*The loss of these artifacts is a terrible tragedy.*

items.question_content:
###Formal language

>*The so-called "ship of the line" was the largest class of sailing ship in the British Royal Navy. Carrying over one hundred cannons and crewed by up to 900 men, a first-rate ship of the line was a nearly unstoppable force in naval battle.*

**Which of the following sentences matches the level of formal language in the rest of the text?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
As we write, we want to make sure the language we use is *consistent*. So, if we're using **formal** language, we should use similar language throughout the entire text.

Similarly, if we're using **casual** language, we shouldn't suddenly add fancy words and complicated sentence structures.

This is a formal academic text focused on the British Navy, so we shouldn't use casual language like "big boats" or "super powerful".

The sentence that matches the rest of the text is

>***These formidable vessels contributed massively to the British Empire's naval dominance.***

choices.content: *Having such big boats helped make the British Navy super powerful.*
*These formidable vessels contributed massively to the British Empire's naval dominance.*
